gez.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *bayt-.

am.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *bayt-. Cognates include Ge'ez ቤት (bet) and Tigrinya ቤት (bet).

syc.etymology: From Aramaic עֵינָא (ʿēnā), from Proto-Semitic *ʿayn-.

aii.etymology: Denominal verb derived from ܥܲܝܢܵܐ (ˁaynā, “eye”), from Proto-Semitic *ʕayn-; compare Arabic عَيَّنَ (ʕayyana) and Hebrew עִיֵּן (iyén).
Inherited from Aramaic עַיְנָא (ʿaynā), from Proto-Semitic *ʕayn-; compare Arabic عَيْن (ʕayn), Hebrew עַיִן ('áyin) and Akkadian 𒅆 (īnum).
Noun of place derived from ܥܲܝܢܵܐ (ˁaynā, “eye; spring”); compare Arabic مَعِين (maʕīn) and Hebrew מַעְיָן (ma'ayán).
Passive participle of the verb ܡܥܲܝܸܢ (mˁayyin) with the sense of “rhombus” a calque of Arabic مُعَيَّن (muʕayyan); compare Hebrew מְעֻיָּן (m'uyán).
Passive participle of the verb ܡܥܲܝܸܢ (mˁayyin) with the sense of “rhombus” a calque of Arabic مُعَيَّن (muʕayyan); compare Hebrew מְעֻיָּן (m'uyán).

aii.gloss: singular construct state of ܥܲܝܢܵܐ (ˁaynā, “eye; spring”)

aii.etymology: The sense of “the same” is a semantic loan from Ottoman Turkish عینی (aynı), from Arabic عَيْن (ʕayn, “eye; the thing itself”).

aii.gloss: third-person single-possession of ܥܲܝܢܵܐ (ˁaynā); his/its eye
aii.etymology: The sense of “the same” is a semantic loan from Ottoman Turkish عینی (aynı), from Arabic عَيْن (ʕayn, “eye; the thing itself”).

he.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *ʕayn-.
From Proto-Semitic *ʕayn-.

he.gloss: to see, view, eye, to lay eyes on
plural construct state form of עַיִן (áyin): (the) eyes of

ar.etymology: Inherited from Proto-Semitic *ʕayn-, from Proto-Afroasiatic *ʕayVn-.

ar.gloss: to be appointed, to be assigned, to be named, to be nominated
to view, to eye, to see with one's eyes, to examine, to inspect, to survey

ar.etymology: Morphologically from the root ع ي ن (ʕ y n).
Cognate with Hebrew מַעְיָן (ma'ayán, “spring, fountain”).

am.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *ʿayn-. Cognates include Arabic عَيْن (ʕayn).

arc.gloss: to be whole, to be complete

arc.etymology: Common Northwest Semitic, Hebrew שָׁלוֹם (šālōm), Phoenician 𐤔𐤋𐤌 (šlm /⁠šalōm⁠/), Ugaritic 𐎌𐎍𐎎 (šlm /⁠šalāmu⁠/).
Common Northwest Semitic, Hebrew שָׁלוֹם (šālōm), Phoenician 𐤔𐤋𐤌 (šlm /⁠šalōm⁠/), Ugaritic 𐎌𐎍𐎎 (šlm /⁠šalāmu⁠/).

syc.etymology: Compare Phoenician 𐤔𐤋𐤌 (šlm), Hebrew שָׁלוֹם (šālōm), Ugaritic 𐎌𐎍𐎎 (šlm /⁠šalāmu⁠/).
From the root ܫ ܠ ܡ related to completing.
Compare Phoenician 𐤔𐤋𐤌 (šlm), Hebrew שָׁלוֹם (šālōm), Ugaritic 𐎌𐎍𐎎 (šlm /⁠šalāmu⁠/).
Compare Hebrew שָׁלֵם (šālēm). The sense of "strong plural" is a calque of Arabic سَالِم (sālim).

syc.gloss: strong plural (as opposed to a broken plural)
syc.etymology: Compare Hebrew שָׁלֵם (šālēm). The sense of "strong plural" is a calque of Arabic سَالِم (sālim).

syc.gloss: peal feminine absolute singular active participle of ܫܠܡ

aii.gloss: to be/become safe, well (mind, body or state)
aii.etymology: Compare Arabic سَلِمَ (salima) and Hebrew שָׁלַם (shalám).

aii.gloss: it is finished; used to mark the end of a writing
aii.etymology: Learned borrowing from Classical Syriac, the Classical Syriac perfect tense of ܫܵܠܹܡ (šālēm, “to be finished”).
Borrowed from Arabic الْإِسْلَام (al-ʔislām); compare also the native ܡܲܫܠܡܵܢܘܼܬ݂ܵܐ (mašlmānūṯā).
Intensitive stem of the verb ܫܵܠܹܡ (šālēm); compare Hebrew שִׁלֵּם (shilém) and Arabic سَلَّمَ (sallama⁩).

aii.gloss: to hand over, turn over, surrender, forsake, betray
aii.etymology: Causitive stem of the verb ܫܵܠܹܡ (šālēm) with the third definition a Semantic loan from Arabic أَسْلَمَ (ʔaslama, “to surrender; betray; convert to Islam”); compare Hebrew הִשְׁלִים (hishlím).
Common Northwest Semitic, from Aramaic שְׁלָמָא (šəlāmā); compare Hebrew שָׁלוֹם (shalóm), Phoenician 𐤔𐤋𐤌 (šlm), Arabic سَلَام (salām) and Ugaritic 𐎌𐎍𐎎 (šlm /⁠šalāmu⁠/).
Common Northwest Semitic, from Aramaic שְׁלָמָא (šəlāmā); compare Hebrew שָׁלוֹם (shalóm), Phoenician 𐤔𐤋𐤌 (šlm), Arabic سَلَام (salām) and Ugaritic 𐎌𐎍𐎎 (šlm /⁠šalāmu⁠/).

Uncertain, possibly related to Akkadian 𒊩𒊓𒄠𒈬𒊏𒆳 (/⁠Shammuramat⁠/), though a direct loan ܫܲܡܝܼܪܵܡ (šamīrām) already exists. More likely related to the root ܫ ܠ ܡ (š l m) related to being complete, perfect, friendly, or whole.

he.gloss: to be safe (mind, body or state)

ar.etymology: From Aramaic שילמא / ܫܱܝܠܡܳܐ (šayləmā), also ܫܱܝܠܳܘܡܳܐ (šaylāmā), too meaning ryegrass, from the root שׁ־ל־ם / ܫ ܠ ܡ related to “rest, tranquility; peace”, as it is used as a sedative in folk medicine and one rests in peace after one intakes this plant inadvertently.

arc.etymology: Cognate with Hebrew עוֹלָם (ʿōlām), Arabic عَالَم (ʕālam).

syc.etymology: From the root ܥ ܠ ܡ. Compare Arabic غُلَيِّم (ḡulayyim) and Hebrew עֶלֶם (ʿélem).
From the root ܥ ܠ ܡ. Compare Arabic غُلَيِّم (ḡulayyim) and Hebrew עֶלֶם (ʿélem).
From ܥܠܝܡܐ (ʿəlaymā, “youth, adolescent”), from the root ܥ ܠ ܡ + the abstract noun ending -ܘܬܐ (-ūṯā).
Compare Arabic عَالَم (ʕālam) and Hebrew עוֹלָם (ʿôlām).

aii.gloss: boy, lad, adolescent, young man, teenager
aii.etymology: Learned borrowing from Classical Syriac, from Aramaic עֳלַיְמָא (ʿŭlaymā); cognate to Arabic غُلَيِّم (ḡulayyim) and Hebrew עֶלֶם (élem).
Learned borrowing from Classical Syriac equivalent to the absolute form of ܥܵܠܡܵܐ (ˁālmā); compare Hebrew עוֹלָם ('olám).

Inherited from Aramaic עָלְמָא (ʿāləmā), itself possibly from Akkadian 𒂖𒆷𒈬𒌋𒀀 (/⁠ellamu, illamu⁠/, “front, before; both temporally and in location; coming before your time or being currently physically before you now”); compare Hebrew עוֹלָם ('olám), Arabic عَالَم (ʕālam) and Amharic ዓለም (ʿaläm).

aii.gloss: forming words pertaining to the world, eternity
aii.etymology: From Proto-Semitic; compare Hebrew ע־ל־ם (ʿ-l-m), Arabic ع ل م (ʕ l m) and غ ل م (ḡ l m)

he.gloss: a young, unmarried man of marriageable age

he.etymology: Cognate with Aramaic עָלְמָא (ʿāləmā), Arabic عَالَم (ʕālam), Amharic ዓለም (ʿaläm).

he.gloss: to make (something) disappear, vanish; to hide, conceal (something)

he.etymology: From עוֹלָם m (olám) + ־י (-i).

he.gloss: Disappearance, vanishing: the act of disappearing.
he.etymology: Action noun of the verb נֶעֱלַם (ne'elám).

ar.gloss: to know, to have knowledge, to be cognizant, to be aware
to inform, to notify (construed with a direct object of the person notified and either a second direct object of the thing notified about or through بِ (bi))
elative degree of عَالِم (ʕālim); elative degree of عَلِيم (ʕalīm):
ar.etymology: Elative of عَلِيم (ʕalīm, “knowing”).

Borrowed from Aramaic עָלְמָא / ܥܳܠܡܳܐ (ʿāləmā) or Hebrew עוֹלָם (ʿōlām), possibly from Akkadian 𒂖𒆷𒈬𒌋𒀀 (/⁠ellamu, illamu⁠/, “front, before; both temporally and in location; coming before your time or being currently physically before you now”).
Derived from the active participle of the verb عَلِمَ (ʕalima, “to know”), from the root ع ل م (ʕ l m).

gez.etymology: Borrowed from Aramaic עָלְמָא / ܥܳܠܡܳܐ (ʿāləmā).

am.etymology: Likely a borrowing from Ge'ez ዓለም (ʿaläm, “world”). Its plural was also borrowed, as ዓለማት (ʿalämat, “universe”).

akk.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *baraḳ- (“lightning”). Cognate with Arabic بَرْق (barq) or Biblical Hebrew בָּרָק (bɔrɔ́q).

syc.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *baraḳ- (“lightning”); compare Arabic بَرْق (barq), Hebrew בָּרָק (bārāq).

aii.etymology: Inherited from Aramaic בְּרַק (bəraq); compare Arabic بَرَقَ (baraqa).

aii.gloss: lightning (high voltage between a cloud and the ground accompanied by a bright flash)
aii.etymology: Inherited from Aramaic בַּרקָא (barqā), from Proto-Semitic *baraḳ- (“lightning”); compare Arabic بَرْق (barq), Hebrew בָּרָק (barák). The latter noun is an occupational noun derived from the former.
Inherited from Aramaic בַּרקָא (barqā), from Proto-Semitic *baraḳ- (“lightning”); compare Arabic بَرْق (barq), Hebrew בָּרָק (barák). The latter noun is an occupational noun derived from the former.
Intensitive stem denominal from ܒܸܪܩܵܐ (birqā, “lightning; electricity”) and causitive stem of the verb ܒܵܪܹܩ (bārēq).

aii.gloss: jewel, precious stone, gem, gemstone (especially an emerald)
aii.etymology: Also attested in Classical Syriac ܒܪܩܐ (bārqā), from Biblical Hebrew בָּרֶקֶת (bāréqeṯ).

aii.gloss: electric, electrical (of or pertaining to electricity, or powered by electricity)
aii.etymology: From ܒܸܪܩܵܐ (birqā, “lightning; electricity”) + -ܝܐ (-āyā, the attributive adjective ending).

aii.gloss: opposite, on the other side, across, over the way from
aii.etymology: Diverged from ܕܲܠܩܘܼܒ݂ܠ (dalquḇl, “against (figurative sense)”) or from ܒ- (b-, “in”)+Aramaic לְקֻבֵּל (“lqūḇel”, literally “opposite”). The pronunciation with /r/ rather than /l/ may be due to influence from the Kurdish loanword ܒܵܪܵܐ (bārā, “side”).

aii.gloss: plum (oval fleshy fruit of dark red or purple colour)
aii.etymology: Learned borrowing from Classical Syriac, from Ancient Greek πραικόκῐον (praikókĭon), ultimately from Latin praecox (“ripe”); compare also borrowed into Arabic بَرْقُوق (barqūq).

he.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *baraḳ-.

he.gloss: Barak (the commander of the army of Deborah in the Old Testament)
he.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *baraḳ-.
Coined by Eliezer Ben-Yehuda based upon Arabic بَرْقِيَّة (barqiyya). See בָּרָק (barák, “lightning”).
Borrowed from Arabic مَبْرُوك (mabrūk, “congratulations”, interjection).

ar.etymology: From the root ب ر ق (b r q). From Proto-Semitic *baraḳ- (“lightning”), although Fronzaroli states that the noun "lightning" may be a deverbal form, in which case only the verb would be inherited.
From the root ب ر ق (b r q). From Proto-Semitic *baraḳ- (“lightning”), although Fronzaroli states that the noun "lightning" may be a deverbal form, in which case only the verb would be inherited.
Morphologically from the root ب ر ق (b r q).

gez.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *baraḳ- (“lightning”). Cognate with Hebrew בָּרַק, Arabic بَرَقَ (baraqa).

arc.etymology: Ultimately from Proto-Semitic *ʾil-. Cognate with the Arabic إله, Hebrew אֵל (ʾēl), אֱלוֹהַּ / אֱלֹהַּ (ʾĕlṓah), אֱלוֹהִים / אֱלֹהִים (ʾĕlōhīm).

syc.etymology: Ligature of ܝ (yodh) and ܗ (heh), the first two letters of the Tetragrammaton יהוה (YHVH), with three points above signifying the three persons of the Trinity, and one point below signifying God's single substance (ousia).
From Proto-Semitic *ʔil-. The doublet ܐܺܝܠ (ʾīyl) was borrowed from Hebrew אֵל (ʾēl).
From Proto-Semitic *ʔil-. The doublet ܐܺܝܠ (ʾīyl) was borrowed from Hebrew אֵל (ʾēl).

aii.etymology: From Aramaic אֱלָהָא (ʾĕlāhā), from Proto-Semitic *ʔil-; compare Arabic إِلٰه (ʔilāh), اللّٰه (allāh), Hebrew אֵל (el), אֱלוֹהַּ (elóah), אֱלוֹהִים (elohím) and Akkadian 𒀭 (ilum). Doublet of ܐܹܝܠ (ēl).

From Aramaic אֱלָהָא (ʾĕlāhā), from Proto-Semitic *ʔil-; compare Arabic إِلٰه (ʔilāh), اللّٰه (allāh), Hebrew אֵל (el), אֱלוֹהַּ (elóah), אֱלוֹהִים (elohím) and Akkadian 𒀭 (ilum). Doublet of ܐܹܝܠ (ēl).

aii.gloss: Literally, “May God be with you”; God be with you, goodbye, farewell
expression of disapproval of a possible future occurrence of an event
aii.etymology: From ܐܲܠܵܗܵܐ (allāhā, “God”) + ܠܵܐ (lā, “not”) + ܥܵܒ݂ܹܕ݂ (ˁāḇēḏ, “do”), literally “May God not do (it)!”.

aii.gloss: phrase used after a reference to a deceased man
aii.etymology: Literally, “May God absolve him.” Also found in Turoyo ܐܰܠܳܗܐ ܡܚܰܣܶܐ ܠܶܗ (aloho mḥase leh).

aii.gloss: a phrase used after a reference to a deceased man or boy
aii.etymology: Clipping of ܐܲܠܵܗܵܐ ܡܲܢܝܸܚ ܠܹܗ ܓܲܘ ܢܘܼܗܪܵܐ ܘܦܲܪܕܲܝܣܵܐ ܥܲܡ ܟܹܐ̈ܢܹܐ ܘܙܲܕܝܼ̈ܩܹܐ (allāhā manyiḥ lēh gaw nuhrā w-pardaysā ˁam kēnē w-zadīqē, literally “May God give him rest in light and paradise with the fair and the righteous”).

aii.gloss: short prayer often used to wish protection and/or give thanks
aii.etymology: Literally, “May God protect you.”

aii.gloss: short prayer used to wish goodness and/or give thanks; thank you
aii.etymology: Literally, “May God give (to) you.”

he.gloss: Form of אֶל (él) including third-person feminine singular personal pronoun as object.
a male given name, equivalent to English Elijah
he.etymology: Shortened from of אֵלִיָּהוּ ('Eliyáhu, “Elijah”).

he.gloss: tail of a fat-tailed sheep (a type of domestic sheep)
he.etymology: From Proto-West Semitic *ʔalyat-. By surface analysis derived from the root אָלָה ('alá, “to be round, i.e. to be thick, fat”), and hence they are named after their composition and appearance.
Cognate with Phoenician 𐤀𐤋 (ʾl), Ugaritic 𐎀𐎍 (ảl), Akkadian 𒌑𒌌 (ul).
Compare Arabic أُولَاءِ (ʔulāʔi).

he.gloss: plural construct state form of אֱלֹהִים (elohím)

ar.etymology: From the root ء ل ه (ʔ l h). Denominal verb from إِلٰه (ʔilāh, “god”), ultimately from Proto-Semitic *ʾil-.

ar.gloss: a god, a deity, a divinity
ar.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *ʾil- with a vocative suffix ـَاه (-āh) which is else only sporadically attested in Arabic but has left its trace in the -ā vocatives of أَب (ʔab, “father”), أَخ (ʔaḵ, “brother”), حَم (ḥam, “father-in-law”). Forms a root ء ل ه (ʔ l h).

From the root ء ل ه (ʔ l h). Widely theorized to be a contraction of الٱِلٰه (al-ilāh, “God”), from الإلٰه (al-ʔilāh, “the deity”) with loss of initial hamza after the definite article ال (al-). Others suggest it is a variant spelling of the more ancient ألّٰه (allāh), the extra ل (l) serving as an emphatic marker to…
Morphologically from the root ء ل ه (ʔ l h).

akk.etymology: Possibly of onomatopoeic origin.

arc.etymology: From the root Proto-Semitic *q-d-m (“to precede, come before”). Compare Arabic قَدَمَ (qadama), Hebrew קדם and Classical Syriac ܩܕܡ.
From the root Proto-Semitic *q-d-m (“to precede, come before”). Compare Arabic قَدَمَ (qadama), Hebrew קדם and Classical Syriac ܩܕܡ.

arc.gloss: before me: first-person singular suffix form of קדם

arc.etymology: Derived from Proto-Semitic *q-d-m (“to precede, come before”).

syc.etymology: From the root ܩ ܕ ܡ related to preceding. Compare Arabic قَدَمَ (qadama) and Hebrew קָדַם (qāḏam).
From the root ܩ ܕ ܡ related to preceding. Compare Akkadian 𒁾𒊕 (qudmu [DUB.SAG]) and Hebrew קֹדֶם (qṓḏem).
From the root ܩ ܕ ܡ related to preceding. Compare Akkadian 𒁾𒊕 (qudmu [DUB.SAG]) and Hebrew קֹדֶם (qṓḏem).
From the root ܩ ܕ ܡ related to preceding. Compare Arabic قِدْم (qidm) and Hebrew קֶדֶם (qéḏem).
From the root ܩ ܕ ܡ related to preceding. Compare Arabic قِدْم (qidm) and Hebrew קֶדֶם (qéḏem).
Compare Arabic مُقَدَّمَة (muqaddama).
Compare Hebrew קַדְמוּת (qaḏəmûṯ).

aii.etymology: From Aramaic קְדַם (qəḏam); related to Arabic قَدَمَ (qadama) and Hebrew קָדַם (kadám).
From Aramaic קֳדָם (qŏḏām); related to Hebrew קֹדֶם (kódem), Akkadian 𒁾𒊕 (qudmu [DUB.SAG]) and Arabic قُدَّامَ (quddāma).
Intensitive and causative stems of the verb ܩܵܕ݂ܹܡ (qāḏēm, “to precede, come before”).
Intensitive and causative stems of the verb ܩܵܕ݂ܹܡ (qāḏēm, “to precede, come before”).

aii.gloss: before, in front of used with verbs of meeting
aii.etymology: Feminine form of ܩܵܕ݇ܡ (qām) and construct state of ܩܵܕ݇ܡܬ݂ܵܐ (qāmṯā). Compare Classical Syriac ܩܘܼܕ݂ܡܲܬ݂ (quḏmaṯ).
ܠ- (l-, “to”) + ܩܵܕ݇ܡܵܐ (qāmā, “front”).

aii.gloss: previous, preceding, prior, old (of an earlier time)
aii.etymology: Inherited from Aramaic קַדִּימָא (qaddīmā); compare Arabic قَدِيم (qadīm).
ܩܲܕ݂ܡܵܐ (qaḏmā, “first, foremost”) + -ܝܐ (āyā, the attributive adjective ending); compare Classical Syriac ܩܲܕ݂ܡܵܝܵܐ (qaḏmāyā) and Turoyo ܩܰܡܳܝܐ (qamoyo).

he.gloss: masculine singular present participle and present tense of קִדֵּם
ancient, old, antique, immemorial, former, primitive
he.etymology: Cognate with Arabic قديم (qadīm, “old, ancient; eternal”).

he.gloss: to advance, to proceed, to progress, to make progress, to move forward

ar.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *q-d-m (“to precede, come before”). Compare Hebrew קדם and Classical Syriac ܩܕܡ.
Elative of قَدِيم (qadīm, “old”).
Derived from the active participle of قَدِمَ (qadima, “to arrive, to come”).
Derived from the active participle of قَدِمَ (qadima, “to arrive, to come”).

ar.gloss: in front of, in the presence of, before
ar.etymology: From Aramaic 𐡒𐡃𐡌 (qd̠ām, qŭd̠ām) / קֳדָם (qŏḏām) / ܩܘܕܡ / ܩܕܵܡ (qəḏām). Compare سَوْفَ (sawfa, marks what is temporally in front), and native أَمَامَ (ʔamāma, “in front of”).
From Aramaic 𐡒𐡃𐡌 (qd̠ām, qŭd̠ām) / קֳדָם (qŏḏām) / ܩܘܕܡ / ܩܕܵܡ (qəḏām). Compare سَوْفَ (sawfa, marks what is temporally in front), and native أَمَامَ (ʔamāma, “in front of”).

gez.etymology: Cognates include Tigre ቀዳም (ḳädam) and Tigrinya ቀዳማይ (ḳädamay).
Cognates include Tigre ቀዳም (ḳädam) and Tigrinya ቀዳማይ (ḳädamay).

ti.etymology: Cognates include Ge'ez ቀዳሚ (ḳädami) and Tigre ቀዳም (ḳädam).

akk.etymology: Unknown.

akk.gloss: to sing (with or without instrumental accompaniment)
akk.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *zamar- (“to make music”). Cognate with Arabic زَمَرَ (zamara, “to play a reed instrument”) and Biblical Hebrew זִמֵּר (zimmer, “to sing”).

syc.etymology: Compare Arabic زَمَرَ (zamara) and Hebrew זִמֵּר (zimmēr).
From the root ܙ ܡ ܪ related to singing. Compare Arabic زَمْر (zamr) and Hebrew זֶמֶר (zémer).

Cognate with Akkadian 𒂵𒈠𒀸 (gamārum, “to bring to an end, to finish”) and Aramaic גמר / ܓܡܪ (“to complete, to perfect”). Compare Arabic جمر (“to collect, to cense”).
Cognate with Akkadian 𒂵𒈠𒀸 (gamārum, “to bring to an end, to finish”) and Aramaic גמר / ܓܡܪ (“to complete, to perfect”). Compare Arabic جمر (“to collect, to cense”).

he.gloss: May you be signed and sealed in the Book of Life; a common greeting on and before Yom Kippur.

Diminutive of ܟܲܠܬ݂ܵܐ (kalṯā, “bride, daughter-in-law”) + -ܘܿ (-ō). Probably originally used an endearing term from the groom’s family to the bride, hence being formed with the diminutive suffix. Compare Turoyo ܟܰܠܘ (kalu, “bride, daughter-in-law”).

From Classical Syriac from Aramaic כלילא (kəlīlā), from Akkadian 𒄃 (GILIM /⁠kilīlu⁠/); from which also borrowed into Hebrew כְּלִיל (kəlîl) , Classical Mandaic ࡊࡋࡉࡋࡀ (klila) and Arabic إِكْلِيل (ʔiklīl).

From Classical Syriac from Aramaic כלילא (kəlīlā), from Akkadian 𒄃 (GILIM /⁠kilīlu⁠/); from which also borrowed into Hebrew כְּלִיל (kəlîl) , Classical Mandaic ࡊࡋࡉࡋࡀ (klila) and Arabic إِكْلِيل (ʔiklīl).

ti.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *ṯamāniy-. Cognates include Ge'ez ሰማንቱ (sämantu), Amharic ስምንት (səmnət) and Tigre ሰማን (säman).

syc.etymology: From Akkadian qanûm.

aii.etymology: From Aramaic קַנְיָא (qanəya), from Akkadian 𒄀 (qanûm); compare Arabic قَنَاة (qanāh), Ancient Greek κάννα (kánna), Hebrew קָנֶה (kané). Doublet of ܩܲܢܝܼܬ݂ܵܐ (qanīṯā, “channel”), ܩܵܢܘܿܢܵܐ (qānōnā, “law, cannon”), and ܟܢܘܼܢܬܵܐ (knuntā, “wrist; plumb line”).
From Arabic قَنَاة (qanāh), itself borrowed from Aramaic קַנְיָא (qanəya, “reed, cane, tube”), from Akkadian 𒄀 (qanûm). Doublet of ܩܲܢܝܵܐ (qanyā, “pen; reed”).

aii.gloss: bottle (container typically made of glass or plastic and having a tapered neck used primarily for holding liquids)
aii.etymology: From Aramaic קַנִינְתָּא (qannīntā), from Ancient Greek καννίον (kanníon), from Latin canna (“tankard, cantharus”); compare also borrowed into Arabic قِنِّينَة (qinnīna).
Possible calque of Arabic قَلَم رَصَاص (qalam raṣāṣ)

aii.gloss: highlighter, highlighter pen (marker pen used to highlight text)

ar.gloss: slave, serf or a particular kind of slave or serf or enslaved possession, one that is kept or acquired
ar.etymology: Connected to ق ن ي (q n y) meaning to acquire or keep; for more see above.

ar.gloss: any Ferula plant, as well as the gum of such a plant (galbanum)
ar.etymology: From the root ق ن ي (q n y) related to acquiring, for the gum acquired. But rather so formed in Ge'ez where the same root exists and a broader meaning in ቀንአት (ḳännəʾät), ቅንአት (ḳənnəʾät), ቀንዓት (ḳännəʿat, “galbanum; stacta”), which to derive vice versa from Arabic is formally dubious. Possibly a phono-semantic matching…

syc.etymology: Compare Hebrew צֶלֶם (ṣélem).

aii.etymology: Intensitive stem derived from the noun ܨܲܠܡܵܐ (ṣalmā, “image”).
Inherited from Aramaic צַלְמָא (ṣaləmā); cognate with Hebrew צֶלֶם (tsélem), Akkadian 𒀩 (ṣalmum) and Arabic صَنَم (ṣanam) by dissimilation of /l/ to /n/.

he.gloss: To photograph, to take a photograph of.
camera (a device for taking still or moving pictures or photographs)
he.etymology: Coined by Hebrew poet, author, and editor Hayim Nahman Bialik, from צַלָּם (tsalám, “photographer”), coined by David Yellin based on צֶלֶם (tsélem, “image, likeness”). Yellin suggested the term *צַלְמוֹנִיָּה (tsalmoniyá), which Bialik wanted to replace because of his dislike for the suffix ־ִיָּה (-iya).
Action noun of צילם / צִלֵּם (tsilém).

he.gloss: figurine, statuette: A small carved or molded figure.

he.etymology: Cognate with Akkadian 𒀩 (ṣalmum), Classical Syriac ܨܠܡܐ (ṣalmā) and, by dissimilation of /l/ to /n/, Arabic صَنَم (ṣanam).
Coined by Jewish educator, writer and scholar David Yellin, based on צֶלֶם (tsélem, “image, likeness”) (above).

gez.etymology: See ጽልመት (ṣəlmät).
Direct cognate or parallel formation to Arabic ظُلْمَة (ẓulma, “darkness”).

syc.etymology: Derived from Arabic مُسْلِم (muslim).
Derived from Arabic مُسْلِمَة (muslima).

aii.etymology: From ܡܲܫܠܝܵܐ (mašlyā, “fork”) + -ܘܿܢܵܐ (-onā, the diminutive suffix).

he.gloss: For example: used to introduce or otherwise mark an example.
he.etymology: From ל־ (l'-) + משל (mashál).

he.gloss: masculine singular present participle and present tense of מָשַׁל (mashál)

he.etymology: From מָשַׁל (mashál, “to rule, to reign, to govern”). Compare with Arabic مُمَثِّل (mumaṯṯil, “representative”).

he.gloss: Governmental; of or pertaining to a government.

he.etymology: Compare Arabic مَثَل (maṯal, “likeness, simile, parable”) and مِثَال (miṯāl, “example, model, allegory”).

he.gloss: singular construct state form of מֶמְשָׁלָה (memshalá)

ar.etymology: Derived from the passive participle of شَلَحَ (šalaḥa, “to take off”).

ar.gloss: a female given name of French origin
ar.etymology: From French Michelle and French Michel. Doublet of ميكايلا (mīkāylā).

am.etymology: A Cushitic borrowing, so also in Gurage and in Tigrinya መሸላ (mäšäla), Tigre ማሼላ (mašela).

ti.etymology: A Cushitic borrowing, also found in Gurage, Amharic ማሽላ (mašəlla), Tigre ማሼላ (mašela).

syc.etymology: From Middle Persian 𐭢𐭥𐭦 (gwc /⁠gōz⁠/, “walnut”). Compare Arabic جَوْز (jawz) and Hebrew אֱגוֹז (ʾĕḡôz).

aii.etymology: Inherited from Aramaic [Term?], from Middle Persian 𐭢𐭥𐭦 (gwc /⁠gōz⁠/, “walnut”); compare Arabic جَوْز (jawz), Persian گوز (gowz) and Hebrew אֱגוֹז (egóz).
Calque of Arabic جَوْز الهِنْد (jawz al-hind, “coconut”, literally “walnut of India”) through Persian جوز هندی (jowz-e hendi, “nutmeg”, literally “Indian walnut”).

he.etymology: Ultimately from French gaz, itself from Dutch gas.

ar.etymology: Verbal noun of تَجَاوَزَ (tajāwaza).
Verbal noun of جَوَّزَ (jawwaza).

syc.etymology: From Ancient Greek μηχανή (mēkhanḗ).

aii.etymology: Possible calque of Arabic مِكْنَسَة كَهْرَبَائِيَّة (miknasat kahrabāʔiyya).

he.gloss: Form of מ־ (mi-) including second-person feminine plural personal pronoun as object.
Form of מִ־ (mi-) including second-person feminine plural personal pronoun as object.

gez.etymology: Cognate with Arabic مَكَان (makān).

am.etymology: Borrowed from Italian macchina, from Latin machina, from Ancient Greek μαχανά (makhaná).

syc.etymology: Compare Hebrew חַבּוּשׁ (ḥabbū́š, “quince”).
Compare ܚܪܦܘܫܬܐ (ḥarpuštā, “dung beetle”).

aii.gloss: to contain (hold inside or include as a part)
aii.etymology: From Aramaic חְבַשׁ (ḥəḇaš); cognate with Arabic حَبَسَ (ḥabasa) and Hebrew חָבַשׁ (khavásh, “to bind, tie”).
Active adjective of ܚܵܒ݂ܹܫ (ḥāḇēš, “to contain; to include”).

ar.gloss: to collect, to earn, to reap
ar.etymology: Back-formation from الْحَبَشَة (al-ḥabaša, “Ethiopia”) due to ideas of getting richer through incense trade. Alternatively variant of حَفَشَ (ḥafaša, “to gather”), which may be illustrated by the same verb in Ge'ez, ሐፈሠ (ḥäfäśä), variating too as ሐበሠ (ḥäbäśä, “to collect”).
اَلْحَبَشَة (al-ḥabaša, “Ethiopia, the Ethiopians”) + ـِيّ (-iyy).

ar.gloss: Abyssinia (former name of Ethiopia: a country and former empire in East Africa; used as an exonym until the mid 20th century)
ar.etymology: Ge'ez ሐበሠት (ḥäbäśät), only attested in Aksumite inscriptions, later replaced with e.g. ብሔረ ግዕዝ (bəḥerä gəʿz), ብሔረ አግዓዝ (bəḥerä ʾägʿaz) and the Ancient Greek exonym ኢትዮጵያ (ʾityop̣ya); originally also meaning both sides of the Red Sea (which Aksum colonized). To its dominion then Old South Arabian 𐩢𐩨𐩦𐩩 (ḥbs²t) referred.…

am.gloss: A member of the Habesha people, typically of Ethiopian or Eritrean heritage.
am.etymology: Borrowed from Ge'ez ሐበሠ (ḥäbäśä, literally “Habesha”), historically referring to the Habesha people — an ethnonym for certain groups in the Horn of Africa, especially in Ethiopia and Eritrea.

ug.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *miʾat-. Compare Hebrew מֵאָה (mēʾā), Aramaic מְאָה (məʾā), Arabic مِئَة (miʔa).

aii.etymology: Semi-learned borrowing from Classical Syriac ܡܐܬܝܢ, from Aramaic מָאתַיִן (māʾṯayin); compare Turoyo ܡܰܬ݂ܶܐ (maṯe), Arabic مِئَتَان (miʔatān), and Hebrew מָאתַיִם (matáyim).

he.gloss: singular construct state form of מֵאָה (me'á)

he.etymology: מִ־ (mi-, “from”) + אֵת ('ét, “with”)

ti.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *miʾat-. Cognates include Amharic መቶ (mäto) and Hebrew מאה.

aii.etymology: From the older form ܙܸܒܵܐ (zibbā); compare Classical Syriac ܐܸܙܒܵܐ, and possibly related to Classical Syriac ܥܸܣܒܵܐ (“grass”).
Partial calque of French grand-père; similar in Hebrew סַבָּא רַבָּא (sába rába) itself borrowed from Aramaic.

ar.gloss: amniotic, related to the fetal membrane

ug.etymology: Cognate with Hebrew מִי (mi) and Phoenician 𐤌𐤉 (my). Compare also Levantine, Egyptian and Hijazi Arabic مين (mīn, “who”).

gez.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *māy- (“water”).

syc.etymology: Compare Hebrew גַּנָּב (gannā́ḇ).
From ܓܢܒܐ (gannāḇā, “thief”), from the root ܓ ܢ ܒ related to stealing + the abstract noun ending -ܘܬܐ (-ūṯā).

An intensified form of ܓܒܪܐ (gaḇrā, “man”) and Inherited from Aramaic גברא, from Proto-West Semitic *gabbār-. The Nun in the spelling serves to distinguish this word from ܓܒܪܐ. Compare Arabic جَبَّار (jabbār) and Hebrew גִּבּוֹר (gibbôr).

An intensified form of ܓܒܪܐ (gaḇrā, “man”) and Inherited from Aramaic גברא, from Proto-West Semitic *gabbār-. The Nun in the spelling serves to distinguish this word from ܓܒܪܐ. Compare Arabic جَبَّار (jabbār) and Hebrew גִּבּוֹר (gibbôr).

aii.gloss: hump or back of a camel, mountain, etc.

Derived from ܓܵܢܸܒ݂ (gāniḇ, “To steal”).
Compare Hebrew גָּנַב (ganáv) and Arabic جَنَّبَ (jannaba, “to put aside, keep away”).
Active noun noun of ܓܵܢܹܒ݂ (gānēḇ, “to steal”) indicating doing something frequently or habitually; compare Hebrew גַּנָּב (gannáv).
Of the noun pattern 1u23ā; compare Classical Syriac ܓܘܼܢܒܵܐ (gunbā).

From the root ج ن ب (j n b). Has the form of an active participle but the corresponding verb جَنَبَ (janaba, “to ward off”) has the wrong meaning.

From ج ن ب (j n b) with the base stem meaning “to go aside”, “to go far off in a direction”, ”to remove to a distance”, “to be remote”, “to change” or “to veer”, “to take off one's clothes” or “to lay them aside”. The direction originates as the name of a positively-viewed warm-moist southerly wind, the humid weather…

Compare Akkadian 𒇻 (immerum), Arabic إِمَّر (ʔimmar, “young lamb”) and Ugaritic 𐎛𐎎𐎗 (ỉmr, “lamb”).

Irregular and original imperative of the verb ܐܵܡܹܪ (āmēr, “to say, to tell”). Also in Classical Syriac ܡܲܪ (mar) and Turoyo ܡܱܪ (mär). Largely replaced by the regular imperative form 12ō3, i.e. ܐ݇ܡܘܿܪ (mōr).

From Aramaic אִימְּרָא / אִמְּרָא (ʾimmərā) / ܐܶܡܪܳܐ (ʾemmərā, “lamb”), absolute state אִימַּר / אִמַּר (ʾimmar) / ܐܶܡܱܪ (ʾemmar), from Akkadian 𒇻 (immerum, “sheep”). aii.gloss: Ur (An ancient Sumerian city in Mesopotamia, in modern Iraq; modern Tell el-Muqayyar)
aii.etymology: Borrowed from Classical Syriac, from Biblical Hebrew, ultimately from Sumerian 𒋀𒀕𒆠 (Urim).

aii.gloss: euro (currency unit of the European Monetary Union)
aii.etymology: Internationalism with spelling contracted from ܐܹܘܪܘܿܦܹܐ (ēwrōpē, “Europe”) and plural form borrowed from Arabic يُورُوهَات (yūrūhāt).

Borrowed from Classical Syriac, probably from ܐܘܿܪ (ōr) + Middle Persian 𐭧𐭥𐭮𐭫𐭥𐭣𐭩 (hwslwdy /⁠Husrōy⁠/); compare also borrowed into Arabic الرُّهَا (ar-ruhā) and Ottoman Turkish اورفه (Urfa). As a given name, it follows the custom of female-given names often including the names of cities.

aii.gloss: Russia (a transcontinental country in Eastern Europe and North Asia)
aii.etymology: Borrowed from Ancient Greek Ῥῶς (Rhôs) with a prosthetic alaph and possible allusion to ܐܘܿܪ (ōr).
Ultimately from Sanskrit वृष (vṛṣa)

aii.gloss: Urmia, Urmi, or Orumiah (a region and city in modern-day northwest Iran)
aii.etymology: Uncertain. Sometimes said to be from ܐܘܿܪ (ōr, “Ur”) + ܡܲܝܵܐ (mayyā, “water”) in reference to the lake Urmia nearby, but considering the presence of /ʒ/ in the formed adjective ܐܘܼܪܡܝܼܙ̰ܢܵܝܵܐ (urmīžnāyā), more likely from the name of Ahura Mazda. If so, a Doublet of ܗܘܿܪܡܝܼܙܕ (hōrmīzd).

Learned borrowing from Classical Syriac ܐܘܪܕܥܐ. From the older form *ܥܦܪܕܥܐ (ʿəp̄ardəʿāʾ); related to Arabic ضِفْدَع (ḍifdaʕ) and Hebrew צְפַרְדֵּעַ (tz'fardéa). Displaced by ܦܸܩܥܵܐ (piqˁā) and ܥܲܩܪܘܼܩܵܐ (ˁaqrūqā).

aii.gloss: Europe (a continent located west of Asia and north of Africa)
aii.etymology: Learned borrowing from Classical Syriac, from Ancient Greek Εὐρώπη (Eurṓpē).

Dialectal form of ܐܲܪܡܠܵܐ (armlā), Inherited from Aramaic [Term?]; compare Classical Syriac ܐܪܡܠܐ (ʾarməlā), Arabic أَرْمَل (ʔarmal) and Hebrew אַלְמָן (almán).

ar.etymology: From the root ع ش ر (ʕ š r), perhaps in the sense of association because the fibrous plant yields cordage for medicinal purposes.

From the root ع ش ر (ʕ š r). Pregnancy of the Arabian camel lasts 390 days, so after ten months a she-camel is called so.
From Proto-Semitic *ʿiśrū-. Compare Hebrew עֶשְׂרִים (ʿeśrīm), Aramaic עֶסְרִין (ʿesrīn) and Ugaritic 𐎓𐎌𐎗𐎎 (ʿšrm).

arc.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *ʔurḫ-. Akin to Old Aramaic 𐤀𐤓𐤇𐤀 (ʾrḥʾ), Jewish Babylonian Aramaic אוֹרְחָא (ʔorḥā), Classical Syriac ܐܘܼܪܚܵܐ (ʾurḥā), Nabataean Aramaic 𐢁𐢛𐢊𐢀 (ʾrḥʾ), Christian Palestinian Aramaic ܐܘܪܚܐ, Jewish Palestinian Aramaic ארחה, Classical Mandaic ࡏࡅࡄࡓࡀ (ʿuhra).

From Proto-Semitic *ʔurḫ-. Akin to Old Aramaic 𐤀𐤓𐤇𐤀 (ʾrḥʾ), Jewish Babylonian Aramaic אוֹרְחָא (ʔorḥā), Classical Syriac ܐܘܼܪܚܵܐ (ʾurḥā), Nabataean Aramaic 𐢁𐢛𐢊𐢀 (ʾrḥʾ), Christian Palestinian Aramaic ܐܘܪܚܐ, Jewish Palestinian Aramaic ארחה, Classical Mandaic ࡏࡅࡄࡓࡀ (ʿuhra).

syc.etymology: From the root ܐ ܪ ܚ related to going or travelling. Compare Biblical Hebrew אוֹרֵחַ (ʾôrḗaḥ).
From Proto-Semitic *ʔurḫ-. Synchronically, from the root ܐ ܪ ܚ related to going or travelling. Compare Hebrew אֹרַח (ʾṓraḥ).

aii.etymology: Inherited from Aramaic אָרְחָא (ʾārḥā), equivalent to the active participle of ܐܵܪܹܚ (ārēḥ, “to journey, to travel”); compare Hebrew אוֹרֵחַ (oréakh). The first sense developed from the second (now displaced by ܐܘܼܪܚܵܝܵܐ (urḥāyā)); where travelers were regularly treated as guests, and guests from other villages would…
Inherited from Aramaic אוּרְחָא (ʾurəḥā), from Proto-Semitic *ʔurḫ-; related to Hebrew אֹרַח (orákh) and Akkadian 𒌫𒄷𒌝 (urḫum).

From ܐܘܼܪܚܵܐ (urḥā, “road, way”) + -ܝܐ (-āyā, the attributive adjective ending). Diverged from ܐܵܪܚܵܐ (ārḥā) after the sense where travellers were regularly treated as guests, and guests from other villages would typically travel long distances.
Active noun of ܡܐܲܪܸܚ (mˀarriḥ, “to host, entertain”).

Inherited from Aramaic רְחֵא/ܪܚܹܐ (rḥē) with a prosthetic alaph, the absolute form of רַחְיָא (raḥyā); compare Classical Syriac ܪܲܚܝܵܐ (“mill, millstone”), notice both having the feminine gender despite a masculine noun structure; compare also Arabic رَحَى (raḥā, “millstone”) and Hebrew רֵחַיִם (rekháyim, “millstone,…

ar.etymology: From قَرَشَ (qaraša, “to gnash, to grind (one's teeth)”). Doubtfully from Ancient Greek καρχαρίας (karkharías, “shark”), from κάρχαρος (kárkharos, “sharp, jagged”), also present in Jewish Babylonian Aramaic כרשא (“probably shark”), Latin carcharias, carcharus.

ar.gloss: Qurayshi, Qurayshite, relating to the tribe of Quraysh
ar.etymology: قُرَيْش (qurayš) + ـِيّ (-iyy); for further etymology see قُرَيْش (qurayš).

Various theories exist. One theory cites the Diminutive form of قِرْش (qirš, “shark, any large fish that eats smaller fish”); the name semantically is parallel to being the top dog, as sharks are apex predators at the top of their food chain. 9th-century scholar Hisham ibn al-Kalbi asserted that the name stemmed from…

ti.etymology: From Arabic قِرْش (qirš). The meaning is from the Maria Theresa thaler.

syc.etymology: From Proto-Semitic *kabid- (“liver”).

he.etymology: Related to כָּבֵד (kavéd, “heavy”).

he.etymology: From לְ־ (l'-, “to”) + כְּבוֹד (k'vod, “honor of”).

he.gloss: a male given name, equivalent to English Ichabod
he.etymology: From אִי (ʾī, “not”) + כָּבוֹד (kāḇōḏ, “honor”), literally "(there is) no honor".

From Proto-Semitic *kabid- (“liver”), which literally means "heavy (one)", since when washing the internal organs of slaughtered animals the liver sinks to the bottom. Compare the similar etymology of Russian лёгкое (ljóxkoje, “lung”), directly from лёгкий (ljóxkij, “light”), because in contrast to the liver the lungs…

ar.gloss: to suffer, sustain, undergo, experience, endure, bear

ar.etymology: From Proto-Semitic. Related to Akkadian 𒅗𒁀𒌈 (kabātum) and Ge'ez ክቡድ (kəbud).
From Proto-Semitic *kabid- (“liver”). Cognate with Biblical Hebrew כָּבֵד (kɔḇeḏ) and Amharic ጉበት (gubät).

am.gloss: to be heavy, to be serious, to be difficult

arc.etymology: Cognate with Arabic دِين (dīn), Amharic ዳኛ (daña), Hebrew דין and Ugaritic 𐎄𐎊𐎐 (dyn).

syc.etymology: From the older form ܐܳܕܺܝܢ not present in Syriac. Influenced heavily by Ancient Greek δέ (dé).

aii.etymology: Inherited from Aramaic דֵּינָרָא (dēnārā), from Ancient Greek δηνάριον (dēnárion), ultimately from Latin dēnārius; compare also borrowed into Arabic دِينَار (dīnār) and Hebrew דִּינָר (dinár).
Borrowed from Arabic دِينَاصَوْر (dīnāṣawr), from French dinosaure, from English dinosaur, which was coined from Ancient Greek δεινός (deinós, “fearsome”) + σαῦρος (saûros, “lizard”).

he.gloss: judgement; law (a written or understood rule)

Cognate with Arabic دَيَّان (dayyān), Aramaic דַּיָּנָא (dayyānā).

he.gloss: a surname, Dayan, originating as an occupation
he.etymology: Cognate with Arabic دَيَّان (dayyān), Aramaic דַּיָּנָא (dayyānā).
Likely borrowed from Aramaic מְדִינְתָּא (məḏīntā), itself formed from the root of דין (“to judge”), denoting the place where judgement takes place. Cognate with Arabic مَدِينَة (madīna, “city”).
Likely borrowed from Aramaic מְדִינְתָּא (məḏīntā), itself formed from the root of דין (“to judge”), denoting the place where judgement takes place. Cognate with Arabic مَدِينَة (madīna, “city”).

ar.etymology: From the root د ي ن (d y n). Compare Hebrew דן.

From the root د ي ن (d y n). Compare Akkadian 𒁲𒋻 (dayyānum), Biblical Hebrew דַּיָּן (dayyān, “judge”), Ge'ez ዳንያ (danya) and Aramaic דינא (dayyānā).
From the root د ي ن (d y n). Compare Akkadian 𒁲𒋻 (dayyānum), Biblical Hebrew דַּיָּן (dayyān, “judge”), Ge'ez ዳንያ (danya) and Aramaic דינא (dayyānā).

am.etymology: Cognate with Arabic دِين (dīn), Aramaic דִּינָא (dīnā), Hebrew דין and Ugaritic 𐎄𐎊𐎐 (dyn).

arc.etymology: Already Imperial Aramaic 𐡆𐡌𐡍𐡀 (zmnʾ), borrowed from a late pronunciation of Old Persian *jamānā (“time”), reconstructible for Proto-Iranian *ǰámaHnaH.

Borrowed from a late pronunciation of Old Persian *jamānā (“time”), much closer to Middle Persian 𐭥𐭣𐭭𐭠 (ODNA /⁠zamān⁠/), 𐭦𐭬𐭠𐭭𐭩(ẕmʾn' /⁠zamān⁠/), 𐭦𐭬𐭭𐭩 (zmn' /⁠zamān⁠/), 𐫉𐫖𐫀𐫗 (zmʾn/⁠zamān⁠/), reconstructible for Proto-Iranian *ǰámaHnaH.

aii.etymology: From a variant of Aramaic זִמְנָא (zimnā), itself either from Old Persian *jamānā or Akkadian 𒋛𒈠𒉡 (si-ma-nu /⁠simānu⁠/) with the meaning of time as an abstract concept from Persian influence; compare Hebrew זְמַן (z'mán), Arabic زَمَان (zamān) and Persian زمان (zamân).
From Aramaic זִמְנָא (zimnā, “set time, instance”) related to ܙܲܒ݂ܢܵܐ (zaḇnā, “time”); cognate to Hebrew הִזְמִין (hizmín)

Semi-denomnial root from ܙܲܒ݂ܢܵܐ (zaḇnā, “time”) and its older variant of Aramaic זִמְנָא (zimnā), itself either from Old Persian *jamānā or Akkadian simānu. Therefore compare Arabic ز م ن (z m n) and Hebrew ז־מ־ן (z-m-n).

From the root ז־מ־ן in puál construction, noun follows from verb.

Borrowed from another pronunciation in Aramaic than زَمَان (zamān).

Cognate with Aramaic נִדְרָא (niḏrā), Arabic نَذْر (naḏr), and Punic 𐤍𐤃𐤓 (ndr).

From the root ܩ ܠ ܠ related to being light. Compare Arabic قَلِيل (qalīl) and Hebrew קַל (qal).

Of the adjective pattern 1a22ū3ā, inherited from Aramaic קַלּוּלָא (qallūlā); related to Classical Syriac ܩܠܝܠܐ (qallīlā), Arabic قَلِيل (qalīl) and Hebrew קַל (kál).

From Proto-Semitic *ṣaɣir-. Cognate with Arabic صَغِير (ṣaḡīr, “small”) and Biblical Hebrew צָעִיר (ṣɔʕír, “young”).
From Proto-Semitic *ṣaɣir-. Cognate with Arabic صَغُرَ (ṣaḡura, “to be(come) small, young”) and Biblical Hebrew צָעַר (ṣɔʿar, “to be small, insignificant”).
From Proto-Semitic *ṣaɣir-. Cognate with Arabic صَغُرَ (ṣaḡura, “to be(come) small, young”) and Biblical Hebrew צָעַר (ṣɔʿar, “to be small, insignificant”).

Form of שַׂהַר (sáhar, śáhar, “moon”). Compare Aramaic סהרא (sahrā, “moon”), Arabic شَهْر (šahr, “month, new moon”), Old South Arabian 𐩦𐩠𐩧 (s²hr, “beginning of the lunar month”), Ge'ez ሣህር (śahr, “moon, first day of the month”).
Probably related to סַהַר (sáhar, “round object”), whence literally, “a round enclosure”.
Internationalism. Ultimately from Arabic صَحَارَى (ṣaḥārā, “deserts”). Direct borrowing from Arabic would render ح (ḥ) as ח (kh, ḥ).

syc.etymology: Compare Akkadian 𒋓 (išku) and Hebrew אֶשֶׁךְ (ʾéšeḵ).

aii.etymology: Compare Classical Syriac ܐܫܟܬܐ (ʾeškəṯā), Akkadian 𒋓 (išku) and Hebrew אֶשֶׁךְ (éshekh).

aii.gloss: shoemaker, cobbler (worker who makes or repairs shoes)
aii.etymology: From Aramaic, from Akkadian 𒀿 (AŠGAB /⁠aškāpu⁠/); compare Arabic إِسْكَاف (ʔiskāf) and Hebrew אֻשְׁכָּף (ʾuškāp̄)

he.etymology: Cognate with Akkadian 𒋓 (iškum), Classical Syriac ܐܫܟܬܐ (ʾeškəṯā) and Ugaritic 𐎜𐎌𐎋 (ủšk).

ar.etymology: Borrowed from Persian اشکانی (aškâni), a corruption of earlier ارشکی (aršaki).
Borrowed from Persian اشکانی (aškâni), a corruption of earlier ارشکی (aršaki).

syc.etymology: Unknown.
From Hebrew אָרוֹן (ʾārôn).
Unknown.

aii.gloss: wardrobe, closet (piece of furniture for storing clothes)
aii.etymology: Learned borrowing from Classical Syriac ܐܪܘܢܐ with the meanings changed, from Biblical Hebrew אָרוֹן (ʾārôn, “coffin, ark”)

he.gloss: Eros (the god of love and sexual desire; son of either Erebus and Nyx or Aphrodite and Ares)
he.etymology: From Ancient Greek Ἔρως (Érōs).
Borrowed from Ancient Greek ἄρωμα (árōma).

he.gloss: plural indefinite form of אֲרוּחָה f (arukhá): meals
plural indefinite form of אָרוֹן (arón): closets, cabinets

gez.etymology: Inherited from Proto-Semitic *ʔarway-. Cognate with Hebrew אריה (“lion”), Amharic አውሬ (ʾäwre, “wild beast”), Ugaritic 𐎀𐎗𐎆 (ảrw, “lion”).

akk.etymology: Cognate with Biblical Hebrew חָדָה (ḥɔḏɔ́, “to rejoice, be glad”).
From 𒄾 (ḫadûm, “to be happy, rejoice”). Compare Biblical Hebrew חֶדְוָה (ḥɛḏwɔ́, “joy”).

arc.etymology: An Iranian borrowing. Compare Persian اندام (andâm) and see there for more.

syc.gloss: body part, limb, member (especially male member)
syc.etymology: From Middle Persian [script needed] (hndʾm /⁠handām⁠/). Compare Persian اندام (andâm) and see there for more.

aii.etymology: From Aramaic הַדָּמָא (haddāmā), from Middle Persian [script needed] (hndʾm /⁠handām⁠/); compare Persian اندام (andâm), Northern Kurdish endam, Armenian անդամ (andam) and Arabic هِنْدَام (hindām).

ar.gloss: to fall down, to break down to collapse
to demolish, to destroy, to wreck
to demolish, to destroy, to wreck

ar.etymology: Literally, “destroyer of pleasures”.
